# Corrective Action Records

**Organisation:** Acme Financial Services Ltd
**Source Audit:** AUDIT-2024-03
**ISO 27001:2022 Reference:** Clause 10.1 (Nonconformity and corrective action)

These records document corrective actions raised following the internal audit (AUDIT-2024-03, conducted September 2024). Two records are presented: CAR-001 (In Progress) and CAR-002 (Closed — Effective).

---

# CAR-001 — Access Review Gap and Orphaned IAM Accounts

## Record Header

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **CAR Reference** | CAR-001 |
| **Status** | In Progress |
| **Severity** | Major Non-Conformity |
| **Source Finding** | F-001 (Audit AUDIT-2024-03) |
| **ISO 27001:2022 Controls** | 5.15 (Access control), 5.18 (Access rights), 9.1 (Monitoring) |
| **Date Raised** | 2024-10-10 |
| **Target Closure Date** | 2024-12-15 |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Review Authority** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Verified by** | To be confirmed on closure |

---

## 1. Non-Conformity Description

The internal audit (AUDIT-2024-03, Finding F-001) identified that no formal review of access rights has been conducted in the 12 months prior to the audit. Three AWS IAM user accounts with production-level permissions (AmazonRDSFullAccess, AmazonEC2FullAccess) were found without a named owner:
- `iam-user-olddev01` — last accessed 2024-02-14; no current owner
- `iam-user-contractor-xyz` — last accessed 2023-11-30; contractor engagement confirmed ended
- `iam-user-svc-monitoring-legacy` — last accessed 2024-04-01; purpose undocumented

The absence of a formal access review process and the presence of unowned accounts with production access represents a major non-conformity against ISO 27001:2022 controls 5.15 and 5.18, and against the monitoring requirements of Clause 9.1.

**Auditor quote (David Hargreaves, AUDIT-2024-03):** *"The presence of unowned accounts with production database and compute access, combined with the absence of any access review discipline, creates material risk of both accidental and malicious misuse."*

---

## 2. Root Cause Analysis

A structured 5-Why root cause analysis was conducted on 2024-10-14, facilitated by Marcus Webb with J. Patel and L. Okonkwo.

### 5-Why Analysis

| Why | Answer |
|-----|--------|
| **Why 1:** Why were there unowned accounts with production access? | The accounts were not revoked when the individuals or processes they served were no longer active |
| **Why 2:** Why were the accounts not revoked when no longer needed? | There was no systematic process to identify and review accounts that had become dormant or orphaned |
| **Why 3:** Why was there no systematic review process? | The access review process was owned by a staff member who left Acme in Q1 2024; the process was not formally handed over to a successor |
| **Why 4:** Why was the process not formally handed over? | The process existed only as tribal knowledge; it was not documented in ISMS-controlled documentation with a named role owner |
| **Why 5:** Why was the process not documented? | When the access review process was initially established, there was no ISMS formal requirement for documented operating procedures; this gap was not identified until the current gap assessment |

### Root Cause Summary

**Immediate cause:** Access review process was assigned to a person who left the company with no succession planning.

**Underlying root cause:** The access review process was not formally documented in the ISMS, had no named backup owner, and had no automated reminders or escalation mechanism. This meant that when the original owner departed, the process simply stopped being performed without anyone noticing.

**Contributing factor:** No Jira automation or calendar-based trigger existed to prompt the access review, so the dependency on human memory was total.

---

## 3. Immediate Containment Actions

The following containment actions were taken immediately following the audit finding, before the corrective action plan was formally established:

| Action | Owner | Completed |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| Manual emergency access review initiated for all production AWS IAM accounts | J. Patel | 2024-10-05 |
| `iam-user-olddev01` — account deactivated (console disabled, access keys deactivated) | J. Patel | 2024-10-05 |
| `iam-user-contractor-xyz` — account deactivated; confirmed with HR (L. Okonkwo) that contractor engagement ended 2023-10-31 | J. Patel | 2024-10-05 |
| `iam-user-svc-monitoring-legacy` — account deactivated; functionality reviewed; no active processes found to be using this account | J. Patel | 2024-10-07 |
| CloudTrail log review performed for all three deactivated accounts — no evidence of malicious activity; access patterns consistent with legacy automated processes | Marcus Webb | 2024-10-07 |
| Emergency access review sign-off document completed (EVD-001) | J. Patel | 2024-10-05 |
| All active AWS IAM accounts re-confirmed with named owners; 2 further accounts updated with correct owner mapping | J. Patel | 2024-10-10 |

**Containment verification:** Emergency review completed; no active production access without a named owner. All three deactivated accounts confirmed inactive with no evidence of misuse. Results documented in EVD-001 (AWS IAM Access Review Q3 2024 — Emergency).

---

## 4. Corrective Action Plan

The following corrective actions are required to prevent recurrence:

### Action 1 — Implement Quarterly Automated Access Review Workflow

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Configure a Jira automation to create access review tasks at the start of each quarter (1 January, 1 April, 1 July, 1 October). Tasks to be assigned to J. Patel (IT Manager) with system owner sub-tasks for each in-scope system |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Target date** | 2024-11-15 |
| **Evidence of completion** | Jira automation configuration screenshot; first automated task created and confirmed active |
| **Jira ticket** | ISMS-0341 |

**Implementation detail:** The Jira automation will create a quarterly access review epic (ISMS project, type: Access Review) containing sub-tasks for each system: AWS IAM, GitHub, Jira, Microsoft 365, Tenable.io, and any additional SaaS applications. Each sub-task is assigned to the named system owner. The epic is configured with a 20-business-day completion window.

### Action 2 — Assign Named Backup Owners for All ISMS Processes

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Update the ISMS process register to include a primary owner and a named backup owner for every ISMS process. Backup owners must be capable of performing the process if the primary owner is unavailable. Document in Confluence ISMS/Governance/Process-Register |
| **Owner** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Target date** | 2024-11-01 |
| **Evidence of completion** | Updated ISMS process register with primary and backup owner columns populated |
| **Jira ticket** | ISMS-0342 |

### Action 3 — Configure Jira Escalation Automation

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Configure Jira automation to escalate overdue access review tasks to the CISO if not completed within the 20-business-day window. Escalation should create a sub-task assigned to Sarah Chen and send an email notification |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager |
| **Target date** | 2024-12-01 |
| **Evidence of completion** | Jira automation configuration; test run demonstrating escalation behaviour |
| **Jira ticket** | ISMS-0343 |

### Action 4 — Document Access Rights Review Procedure

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Create an ISMS-controlled Access Rights Review Procedure (PROC-ARR-001) documenting the quarterly review process, system scope, evidence requirements, sign-off authorities, and escalation steps |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager (draft); Sarah Chen, CISO (approve) |
| **Target date** | 2024-12-15 |
| **Evidence of completion** | PROC-ARR-001 approved and published in Confluence |
| **Jira ticket** | ISMS-0344 |

---

# CAR-002 — Vulnerability Management — No SLA, Unresolved Critical/High Findings

## Record Header

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **CAR Reference** | CAR-002 |
| **Status** | Closed — Effective |
| **Severity** | Major Non-Conformity |
| **Source Finding** | F-002 (Audit AUDIT-2024-03) |
| **ISO 27001:2022 Controls** | 8.8 (Management of technical vulnerabilities), 9.1 (Monitoring) |
| **Date Raised** | 2024-10-10 |
| **Date Closed** | 2024-11-22 |
| **Target Closure Date** | 2024-11-30 |
| **Closed Early** | Yes — 8 days ahead of target |
| **Owner** | Marcus Webb, Head of Information Security |
| **Review Authority** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Effectiveness Verified by** | David Hargreaves, Lead Auditor (External) — 2024-11-27 |

---

## 1. Non-Conformity Description

The internal audit (AUDIT-2024-03, Finding F-002) identified that Tenable.io vulnerability scanning was operational but:
1. No formal vulnerability remediation SLA had been defined or documented
2. 14 findings rated Critical or High severity were unresolved for more than 90 days (as at the 2024-08-01 scan report)
3. No corresponding Jira remediation tickets existed for 12 of the 14 findings
4. No escalation mechanism existed for overdue findings

Specific unresolved findings included two Critical-severity CVEs on production EC2 instances hosting the payment API.

**Auditor quote (David Hargreaves, AUDIT-2024-03):** *"Unresolved Critical and High severity vulnerabilities on production systems, with no SLA and no remediation tracking, represent a serious gap. The payment processing API runs on infrastructure affected by these findings."*

---

## 2. Root Cause Analysis

Root cause analysis conducted 2024-10-15 by Marcus Webb with J. Patel.

### 5-Why Analysis

| Why | Answer |
|-----|--------|
| **Why 1:** Why were 14 Critical/High vulnerabilities unresolved for >90 days? | No formal remediation SLA existed; there was no mechanism to identify or escalate overdue findings |
| **Why 2:** Why was there no SLA? | The vulnerability management process was informal; Tenable.io was deployed for scanning but no remediation workflow was established alongside it |
| **Why 3:** Why was there no remediation workflow? | Tenable.io was procured to satisfy a PCI DSS scanning requirement; the process design stopped at "scan and review" rather than "scan, ticket, remediate, verify" |
| **Why 4:** Why was the workflow not designed end-to-end? | No one was explicitly accountable for the vulnerability management programme end-to-end; scanning was owned by IT, but remediation fell between IT and Engineering without a clear owner |
| **Why 5:** Why was end-to-end ownership not assigned? | The ISMS roles and responsibilities (control 5.2) are incomplete; control 8.8 had no named owner in the ISMS at the time |

### Root Cause Summary

**Immediate cause:** No formal remediation SLA and no ticketing integration meant findings were not tracked or escalated.

**Underlying root cause:** Tenable.io was procured and deployed for scanning compliance purposes, but the full vulnerability management lifecycle (scan → ticket → remediate → verify → report) was never designed or documented. Accountability for end-to-end vulnerability management was not formally assigned.

---

## 3. Immediate Containment Actions

Emergency review of all Critical and High severity Tenable.io findings was conducted on 2024-10-03:

| Action | Owner | Completed |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| Emergency Tenable.io scan run to get current finding status | J. Patel | 2024-10-03 |
| All 14 unresolved findings reviewed with Engineering leads | Marcus Webb, Lead Engineer | 2024-10-03 to 2024-10-05 |
| 11 of 14 findings remediated (patches applied, configurations updated) | Cloud Engineering | 2024-10-17 |
| 3 remaining findings accepted as residual risk with documented business risk acceptance | Sarah Chen, CISO | 2024-10-18 |
| Risk acceptance forms completed for 3 accepted findings (RISK-005 updated to reflect treatment) | Marcus Webb | 2024-10-18 |
| Emergency scan re-run to verify remediation | J. Patel | 2024-10-20 |

**Risk acceptance detail for 3 remaining findings:**
- Finding RA-001: CVE affecting an EC2 instance scheduled for decommission by 2024-11-30; mitigation: network access restricted to internal only; risk accepted by CISO until decommission
- Finding RA-002: CVE in a third-party library with no available patch; mitigation: WAF rule added; risk accepted by CISO pending vendor patch
- Finding RA-003: Low-exploitability CVE on monitoring EC2 instance; mitigation: instance isolated from CDE; risk accepted

---

## 4. Corrective Action Plan

### Action 1 — Define Vulnerability Remediation SLAs in Vulnerability Management Procedure

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Draft, review and approve a Vulnerability Management Procedure (PROC-VM-001) defining formal remediation SLAs: Critical: 24 hours; High: 7 calendar days; Medium: 30 calendar days; Low: 90 calendar days. Define the risk acceptance process for findings that cannot be remediated within SLA |
| **Owner** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Approver** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Target date** | 2024-11-15 |
| **Jira ticket** | SEC-1101 |

### Action 2 — Integrate Tenable.io with Jira for Automatic Ticket Creation

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Configure the Tenable.io → Jira integration to automatically create SEC project tickets for new Critical and High findings. Tickets must include: CVE reference, severity, affected asset, CVSS score, recommended remediation, and SLA due date |
| **Owner** | J. Patel, IT Manager (technical implementation); Marcus Webb (configuration and testing) |
| **Target date** | 2024-11-01 |
| **Jira ticket** | SEC-1102 |

### Action 3 — Establish Weekly Vulnerability Review Meeting

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Establish a standing weekly vulnerability review meeting (30 minutes, every Tuesday 10:00 UTC). Attendees: Marcus Webb, J. Patel, Lead Engineer. Agenda: new findings, approaching SLA breaches, completed remediations, risk acceptances |
| **Owner** | Marcus Webb, Head of InfoSec |
| **Target date** | 2024-10-22 (first meeting) |
| **Jira ticket** | SEC-1103 |

### Action 4 — Assign Named Owner for Control 8.8 in ISMS

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Action** | Update the ISMS control register to assign Marcus Webb as named owner for control 8.8. Update RACI matrix |
| **Owner** | Sarah Chen, CISO |
| **Target date** | 2024-11-01 |
| **Jira ticket** | ISMS-0350 |

---

---

## 6. Effectiveness Verification

**Verified by:** David Hargreaves, ISO 27001 Lead Auditor (External Consultant)
**Verification date:** 2024-11-27
**Verification method:** Remote spot-check via screen share with Marcus Webb and J. Patel

### Verification Checks Performed

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| Vulnerability Management Procedure (PROC-VM-001 v1.0) reviewed — confirms SLAs and process | Pass — procedure complete, signed, and published |
| Tenable.io → Jira integration verified — 10 recent findings checked for corresponding Jira tickets | Pass — all 10 findings had corresponding tickets created within 30 minutes of scan completion |
| SLA compliance check — 8 High/Critical findings from the past 4 weeks checked against Jira due dates | Pass — all 8 findings either remediated within SLA or have approved risk acceptance documentation |
| Zero findings >90 days old without risk acceptance | Pass — no unaccepted findings >7 days for High, >24h for Critical |
| Weekly vulnerability review meeting attendance records reviewed (5 weeks) | Pass — meetings held weekly; Marcus Webb, J. Patel, and Lead Engineer attended all 5 sessions |
| EC2 instance subject to RA-001 decommission status checked | Pass — instance confirmed decommissioned on 2024-11-28 |

### Auditor Effectiveness Statement

> *I have reviewed the corrective actions taken in response to Finding F-002 from AUDIT-2024-03. The Vulnerability Management Procedure is comprehensive and appropriate. The Tenable.io → Jira integration is operational and functioning correctly. The weekly review meeting is established and attended. A spot-check of 10 findings confirms that the new SLA is being met in practice. The four-week post-implementation period is sufficient to demonstrate process embedding.*
>
> *I am satisfied that the corrective actions are effective and that the non-conformity has been resolved. CAR-002 may be closed.*
>
> **David Hargreaves, ISO 27001 Lead Auditor**
> **2024-11-27**
